unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to the last unit of Year 3! This unit is all about the world inside your head — your thinking, your feelings, and your imagination. You will learn words for ideas and emotions, like idea, sense, reason, sadness, and experience. You will learn how to imagine new things, how to make a choice, and how to suggest ideas kindly to your friends. You will read about children in a coastal East African town who fill a special Box of Ideas with their dreams and hopes. By the end, you will be able to talk about what you think and feel, and use your imagination to write your own ideas down.
unit.learningPath:
Start with the Vocabulary file. Learn a few feeling-and-thinking words each day.
Work through the mini-lessons in the Grammar file (abstract nouns, modal verbs, imperatives, and more).
Read the Story file, “The Box of Ideas”, and answer the questions.
Work through the four skills below: Reading & Listening → Speaking → Writing → Activities.
Finish with the Self-Assessment, then celebrate — you will have finished Year 3!

visual.alt: A child imagining ideas and expressing thoughtful feelings

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A thought or plan in your mind.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I have an idea for a fun game we can play.
practiceSentences: I have an idea for a fun game we can play.
Samira's idea made the whole group smile.
Whose idea was the classroom reading tent?
Adam said, "I have an idea for our poster."
My best idea came while I was walking home.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'idea', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To make a picture of something in your mind.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I can imagine a big, colourful dragon flying in the sky.
practiceSentences: I can imagine a big, colourful dragon flying in the sky.
Close your eyes and imagine a silver river.
Can you imagine living on a tall mountain?
Maya said, "I imagine castles when I read."
I imagine my story before I write anything down.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: i - m - a - g - i - n - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'imagine', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The cause of something, or why it is done.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I have a reason for being late; my bus was slow.
practiceSentences: I have a reason for being late; my bus was slow.
There is a good reason for every class rule.
What is the reason the birds fly south?
Theo said, "The reason I smiled was your joke."
My reason for saving money is a new book.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: r - e - a - s - o - n
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'reason', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The reason why something is made or done.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: The purpose of a pencil is to help us write.
practiceSentences: The purpose of a pencil is to help us write.
The purpose of a lighthouse is guiding ships safely.
What is the purpose of a spider's web?
Sami said, "The purpose of practice is getting better."
My notebook's purpose is holding all my story ideas.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: p - u - r - p - o - s - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'purpose', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A decision between two or more things.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: You can have a cookie or an apple; it is your choice.
practiceSentences: You can have a cookie or an apple; it is your choice.
Reading first was a good choice for Samira.
What choice will you make, football or drawing?
Dad said, "Take your time because it is your choice."
My choice for the trip snack is fresh fruit.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: c - h - o - i - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'choice', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: A way to feel or understand something.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I had a sense that it might rain soon.
practiceSentences: I had a sense that it might rain soon.
The dark clouds gave us a sense of coming rain.
Do you have a sense of which way is home?
Maya said, "I had a sense we would win."
My sense of smell leads me to Mum's baking.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'sense', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: The feeling you have when you are unhappy.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She felt sadness when her friend moved away.
practiceSentences: She felt sadness when her friend moved away.
Sadness filled the class when the holidays ended.
How do you chase sadness away on hard days?
Theo said, "Talking to Mum makes my sadness smaller."
I felt sadness when my best friend was ill.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - a - d - n - e - s - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'sadness', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Something that happens to you that you remember.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I had a fun experience at the zoo.
practiceSentences: I had a fun experience at the zoo.
Riding a camel was a new experience for Samira.
What was your favourite experience this school year?
Adam said, "The boat trip was an amazing experience."
My first day at school was a happy experience.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - x - p - e - r - i - e - n - c - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'experience', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Honest and truly meaning what you say.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She gave me a sincere smile when I helped her.
practiceSentences: She gave me a sincere smile when I helped her.
A sincere apology comes from the heart.
Was his thank-you sincere and true?
Grandma said, "A sincere friend tells you the truth."
I wrote a sincere note to thank my teacher.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - i - n - c - e - r - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'sincere', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Kind and caring about other people.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: She gave her friend a gift because she was thoughtful.
practiceSentences: She gave her friend a gift because she was thoughtful.
The thoughtful boy saved a seat for his friend.
Was it thoughtful of Maya to share her umbrella?
Mum said, "A thoughtful guest brings a small gift."
I was thoughtful and carried Grandma's shopping home.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: t - h - o - u - g - h - t - f - u - l
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'thoughtful', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: Feeling like nothing will get better.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I felt hopeless when I could not find my lost toy.
practiceSentences: I felt hopeless when I could not find my lost toy.
The team felt hopeless until the very last goal.
Do you feel hopeless when a puzzle is hard?
Sami said, "I felt hopeless, but Dad helped me try again."
I never stay hopeless because I ask for help.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: h - o - p - e - l - e - s - s
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'hopeless', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination.

dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: Playing games with friends is very enjoyable.
practiceSentences: Playing games with friends is very enjoyable.
The trip to the farm was truly enjoyable.
Which game is most enjoyable at break time?
Samira said, "Baking bread with Grandma is enjoyable."
I find quiet reading very enjoyable after lunch.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: e - n - j - o - y - a - b - l - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'enjoyable', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To let someone do something; to give permission.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: My mother will allow me to play after my homework.
practiceSentences: My mother will allow me to play after my homework.
The rules allow quiet talking during group work.
Will Dad allow us to camp in the garden?
The librarian said, "We allow four books each visit."
My parents allow me one sweet after dinner.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'allow', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To tell someone an idea or plan.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I suggest we play outside after lunch.
practiceSentences: I suggest we play outside after lunch.
Teachers suggest reading a little every single night.
What game do you suggest for break time?
Adam said, "I suggest we start with the puzzle."
I suggest mangoes for our class fruit salad.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: s - u - g - g - e - s - t
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'suggest', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination.

dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I enjoy reading stories about animals.
practiceSentences: I enjoy reading stories about animals.
The children enjoy story time under the big tree.
Do you enjoy walks along the beach?
Maya said, "I enjoy helping in the kitchen."
I enjoy quiet mornings with my colouring book.
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'enjoy', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination.

dictionaryLinks.childMeaning: To feel sure that something is true or good.
dictionaryLinks.exampleSentence: I believe that kindness makes the world better.
practiceSentences: I believe that kindness makes the world better.
We believe honesty is better than winning.
Do you believe our team can win tomorrow?
Theo said, "I believe you can solve it."
I believe my little sister will read soon.
dictionaryLinks.spellingPractice: b - e - l - i - e - v - e
dictionaryLinks.aiTutorPrompt: Ask me to define 'believe', use it in a sentence, and connect it to Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination.

rubrics.level4: Uses target words accurately and naturally

rubrics.level1: Capital letters and punctuation need support

outcomes.learningOutcome: Use abstract nouns like idea, reason, experience, and sadness to talk about thoughts and feelings.
Say what you imagine and describe pictures in your mind.
Give a reason for something using because.
Use modal verbs like can and will to talk about what is possible and what you plan to do.
Suggest ideas and allow others to speak in a group.
Tell someone what another person said, using reported speech.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Answer opinion questions such as “What do you believe?”
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

outcomes.learningOutcome: Write about a feeling, a memory, or something you imagine.
outcomes.evidenceOfLearning: Independent writing, presentation or project evidence

readings.title: The Box of Ideas at School
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Our teacher brought a bright box to class. “This is a Box of Ideas,” she said. “Every child will add one idea. It can be a dream, a memory, or a hope.” My friend Isaac had a good idea, but he was not sure about its purpose. “Imagine something better,” I told him. So he closed his eyes and thought. His idea was about flying over the sea. It was a thoughtful and sincere idea, and the whole class smiled.

readings.title: Feelings Are Not Bad or Good
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Every day we feel many things. Sometimes we feel joy, and sometimes we feel sadness. Sadness is not a bad thing — it is a feeling, like rain is a kind of weather. When Sophia felt hopeless because her cat was lost, she talked to her mother. Her mother listened with a sincere heart. “It is good to share how you feel,” she said. “When we talk about sadness, we help each other. That is a kind and thoughtful thing to do.” Soon, Sophia felt hopeful again.

readings.title: A Group Discussion
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Teacher: “Today we plan our class garden. Please suggest an idea, and allow everyone to speak.”
Isaac: “I suggest we grow tomatoes, because we can eat them at lunch.”
Selma: “That is a good idea. I think we should grow flowers too, so the garden looks beautiful.”
Teacher: “Thank you both. Those are thoughtful choices.”

readings.title: What Isaac Said
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript: Isaac said, “Sometimes I imagine that I can fly.” Later, his friend told her mother: “Isaac said he could imagine flying. He said it made him feel free.”

readings.title: The Box of Ideas
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
At a school near the sea, the teacher, Macallimad Zainab, stood at the front of the class holding a bright, colourful box. The children leaned forward to look.
“This,” she said, “is our Box of Ideas. Each of you will add one idea. It can be a dream, a memory, or a hope.”
“Why?” asked Isaac.
“Because ideas help us grow,” the teacher said. “They come from feeling, thinking, and listening. Every idea has a reason.”
The next day, Isaac sat quietly under a tree in the schoolyard. He wrote something on a small paper and folded it. Then he looked around, but he did not smile.
“What is the matter?” asked his classmate, Amal.
“I don't think my idea is good,” said Isaac. “I don't even know its purpose.”
“Try again,” she said kindly. “Imagine something better.”
Amal showed him what she had written: “One day I will write music with the sense of rain and drums. It will be enjoyable, like dancing at the beach.”
When Isaac placed his paper in the box, he still felt unsure.
Later, the whole class sat together in a circle. The teacher opened the box and read the first paper: “I want a garden where children feel peace.”
“What a lovely choice,” said Amal. “That is so thoughtful.”
The teacher read another: “I would allow everyone to play, no matter what.”
“That shows a kind purpose,” said the teacher, smiling.
Then she read one more: “I once felt hopeless when my father was ill. But he got better, and now I plant trees with him.” The room went very quiet.
“That is very sincere,” the teacher said softly.
Amal nodded. “That story has a strong reason.”
At last the teacher reached Isaac's paper. She read: “Sometimes I imagine that I can fly. I would fly over the sea and wave at my grandfather's house. I don't know what it means. But I like the feeling.”
The whole class smiled. Amal clapped softly. “That is the best idea yet,” she said.
After class, the teacher said, “Isaac, you shared something thoughtful. That is what imagination is for.” Isaac smiled at last.
That evening, Amal and Isaac walked home together along the shore. “I suggest we make a music box next,” said Amal.
“What would it play?” asked Isaac.
“Whatever we feel,” she said.
The next morning, the teacher asked the class a question: “What do you believe — should we share our ideas, even when we feel sadness?”
“Yes,” said Isaac. “Sadness can help others feel understood.”
The teacher smiled. “Then you have all learned something important.”
The children wrote in their journals. Isaac wrote: “I learned that an experience does not need to be big to have a reason. I will keep writing and imagining.” Amal wrote: “I will try to be more sincere when I share.”
At the end of the week, the box was full. “Now we will seal it and open it again next year,” the teacher said.
“Why?” asked Isaac again.
“Because then you will see how your ideas and choices have grown.”
As the children walked out into the warm afternoon, Isaac whispered, “I used to feel unsure. Now I believe that imagination is a kind of flying.”
Amal laughed. “And we can fly anytime,” she said, “with a good idea.”

grammar.title: Abstract Nouns
grammar.explanation: An abstract noun is a word for something you cannot see, touch, or hold — like a feeling, a quality, or an idea. Words like idea, experience, sadness, and kindness are abstract nouns. They let you talk about what is inside your mind and heart.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Name the feeling or idea:  “She shared an idea.”
Add a describing word to make it stronger:  “She shared a brilliant idea.”
More examples:  “I had a strange experience.”  ·  “His kindness made a difference.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: forgetting that a feeling word is a noun. “She is very sad feeling.”  ✗
Fix: use the abstract noun: “She felt great sadness.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Abstract nouns are like clouds — you cannot touch them, but you can still see their shape. Ask yourself: “Can I touch it?” If not, it is probably an abstract noun.
grammar.practice: Fill in each blank with an abstract noun from the word bank: courage, happiness, idea, sadness. | His ______ helped him face his fears. | She felt pure ______ when she got the good news. | The teacher praised the student's brilliant ______. | He felt deep ______ when his friend moved away.

grammar.title: Modal Verbs (can, will, should, must)
grammar.explanation: Modal verbs are little helper verbs that tell us what is possible, what we are able to do, what we are allowed to do, or what we plan to do. They are important because they add meaning to our sentences.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Use this pattern:  subject + modal verb + base verb (the plain verb, with no “s” and no “to”).
can → able to:  “I can imagine a better world.”
will → a future plan:  “We will share more ideas.”
should → a good idea:  “We should help the new student.”
must → something you have to do:  “You must listen carefully.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: adding “s” to the modal or the verb. “He cans swim.” / “She will swims.”  ✗
Fix: modals never change, and the verb stays plain: “He can swim.” / “She will swim.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Modal verbs are “helpers”. A helper is always followed by the plain verb — never “to”, never an extra “s”.
grammar.practice: Choose the best modal verb. | You ______ (can / must) go to bed early tonight — it is a rule. | We ______ (could / should) try to help the new student. | One day, people ______ (will / must) share more ideas. | ______ (Can / Should) you imagine a world with no sadness?

grammar.title: Imperatives for Instructions
grammar.explanation: An imperative is a sentence that gives an instruction, a command, or a friendly request. We use imperatives to tell someone what to do in a clear, direct way — like in a group discussion or a list of steps.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with the plain verb. You do not need “you”:  “Allow others to speak.”
Add details to make it clear:  “Suggest a helpful idea for the project.”
For a “do not” command, start with Don't:  “Don't interrupt when someone is talking.”
To be polite, add please:  “Please listen carefully.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: adding “you” or using the wrong verb form. “You allowing others to speak.”  ✗
Fix: just start with the plain verb: “Allow others to speak.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Think of an imperative as a friendly order to a friend: clear and helpful, but never bossy.
grammar.practice: Write the correct imperative using the verb in brackets. | ______ (help) your classmates when they ask. | ______ (not interrupt) when someone else is talking. | ______ (share) your ideas with the group. | ______ (be) respectful to everyone.

grammar.title: Countable and Uncountable Nouns
grammar.explanation: Countable nouns are things you can count, like ideas — one idea, two ideas. Uncountable nouns cannot be counted one by one, like sadness or water. Knowing the difference helps you choose the right words for “how much” and “how many”.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Countable: use a / an, numbers, or many:  “a reason”, “three ideas”, “many choices”.
Uncountable: use some or much, and no “a/an”:  “some water”, “much experience”.
Countable nouns can be plural (add s):  one idea → two ideas. Uncountable nouns do not change.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: “I have a water.” / “She has much ideas.”  ✗
Fix: “I have some water.” / “She has many ideas.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Countable nouns you can drop into a bag and count one by one. Uncountable nouns are like liquids or feelings — you cannot split them into single pieces.
grammar.practice: Choose the correct words. | I have ______ (a reason / some reasons) to be happy. | There is ______ (many water / some water) in the glass. | She has ______ (three experiences / much experience) with drawing.

grammar.title: Reported Speech
grammar.explanation: Reported speech is how we tell someone what another person said, without using their exact words. Instead of “He said, ‘I feel hopeless,’” we say, “He said he felt hopeless.” This lets you share other people's ideas and feelings.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
Start with a reporting verb like said or told.
Change the pronoun to fit: “I” becomes “he” or “she”.
Change the verb to the past: “feel” becomes “felt”, “can” becomes “could”, “will” becomes “would”.
Take away the quotation marks.
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: keeping the present tense and the quote marks. “She said, ‘My choice is thoughtful.’”  ✗
Fix: “She said her choice was thoughtful.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Remember three steps: change the pronoun, change the tense, and drop the quotes.
grammar.practice: Rewrite each sentence in reported speech. | Direct: “I love ice cream.”  →  Reported: He said ______. | Direct: “She will come to the game.”  →  Reported: She said ______. | Direct: “We are happy.”  →  Reported: They said ______.

grammar.title: Conjunctions (and, but, or, so, because) + Simple Past in Stories + The Future with “Will” + Opinion Questions
grammar.explanation: Conjunctions are joining words. They glue two ideas together so your sentences flow. The most useful ones are and, but, or, so, and because.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
and adds:  “I like thinking and dreaming.”
but shows a difference:  “I like the idea, but it is hard.”
or offers a choice:  “Do you want juice or milk?”
so shows a result:  “It was raining, so we stayed inside.”
because gives a reason:  “I enjoy thinking because it helps me learn.”
grammar.commonMistake:
Mistake: using the wrong joining word. “I enjoy thinking, but it helps me learn.”  ✗
Fix: “but” shows a difference, not a reason. Use because: “I enjoy thinking because it helps me learn.”  ✓
grammar.memoryTip: Conjunctions are glue for your ideas. Pick the glue that matches: adding, contrasting, choosing, result, or reason.
grammar.practice: Fill in each blank with: and, but, or, so, or because. | I like video games, ______ my brother prefers board games. | We wanted to go to the park, ______ it was too hot. | He studied all night, ______ he wanted to pass the test. | She enjoys painting ______ it helps her relax. | Lesson 7:  Simple Past in Stories | What it means and why it matters | The simple past tense tells about things that already happened. We use it all the time in stories, so the reader knows the actions are finished. | How to form it | Most verbs add -ed:  imagine → imagined  ·  allow → allowed  ·  play → played. | Some verbs are irregular and change shape:  go → went  ·  see → saw  ·  have → had. | A common mistake | Mistake: “She imagine a better world.” / “He goed to the store.”  ✗ | Fix: “She imagined a better world.” / “He went to the store.”  ✓ | Memory tip | Most verbs just need -ed. A few “special” (irregular) verbs must be learned by heart — like went, saw, and had. | Practice 7 | Write the simple past of the verb in brackets. | Yesterday, she ______ (imagine) a magical land. | Last week, he ______ (allow) his friend to join the game. | They ______ (see) a beautiful sunset. | Lesson 8:  The Future with “Will” | What it means and why it matters | We use will to talk about the future — our plans, promises, and predictions. It is like making a little promise about what is going to happen. | How to form it | Pattern:  subject + will + plain verb:  “I will play.”  ·  “She will read a book.” | Negative: add not (won't):  “I will not forget.” | Question: put will first:  “Will you come to the party?” | A common mistake | Mistake: “I will going.” / “You will play?”  ✗ | Fix: use the plain verb, and put “will” first in questions: “I will go.” / “Will you play?”  ✓ | Memory tip | Think of will as a promise about the future. After the promise, always use the plain verb. | Practice 8 | Complete each sentence with “will” and the verb in brackets. | She ______ (play) the piano at the concert. | They ______ (not / go) to the beach tomorrow. | I ______ (help) you with your homework. | Lesson 9:  Opinion Questions | What it means and why it matters | Opinion questions ask what someone thinks or feels. They help us learn about each other and share our own ideas. This unit uses them a lot, like “What do you believe?” | How to form it | Use What for an open opinion:  “What do you think about school?” | Use Is / Are for a yes/no opinion:  “Is sadness always a bad thing?” | Use Do for beliefs:  “Do you believe in kindness?” | A common mistake | Mistake: leaving out the question word. “You like the idea?”  ✗ | Fix: start with a question word: “Do you like the idea?”  ✓ | Memory tip | Remember the start words: What · Is · Should · Do. Begin your opinion question with one of them. | Practice 9 | Choose the correct question word. | ______ (What / Where) do you think about summer holidays? | ______ (Is / Are) homework always necessary? | ______ (Do / Did) you believe that everyone should be allowed to play?

speaking.title: Speaking 1 — Share an Idea
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Get ready to say your idea and your reason:
“I have an idea. I suggest we ______.”
“The reason is that ______.”
“What do you think about my idea?”
Record yourself. Check: Did you say your idea clearly? Did you give a reason with because? Did you invite the other person to answer?
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise share an idea and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 2 — Talk About Your Feelings
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Practise talking about a feeling. It is brave and healthy to share how you feel.
“Yesterday I felt ______ because ______.”
“When I feel sadness, I like to ______.”
“Something that makes me feel hopeful is ______.”
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise talk about your feelings and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 3 — Imagine It!
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines:
Play with your AI tutor or your family:
One person says: “Imagine you can ______ (fly / talk to animals / be invisible).”
The other person answers: “If I could ______, I would ______, because ______.”
Then swap turns. Try to use a new idea each time!
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise imagine it! and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 4 — Vocabulary Expert Interview
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Choose six unit words. Ask and answer a clear question about each word using complete sentences.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise vocabulary expert interview and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 5 — Explain and Respond
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Explain one important unit idea, give a reason with because, and respond to two follow-up questions.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise explain and respond and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

speaking.title: Speaking 6 — One-Minute Unit Talk
speaking.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
speaking.instructionsAndModelLines: Plan and record a one-minute talk using an opening, three connected details, and a closing sentence.
speaking.aiTutorPrompt: Help me practise one-minute unit talk and give feedback on clarity, vocabulary and complete sentences.

writing.title: Writing 1 — My Idea for the Box
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Imagine you can add one idea to the Box of Ideas. Write three or four sentences about your dream, memory, or hope. Try to use the words idea, imagine, and because.
Start like this:  “My idea is ______. I imagine ______. This is important to me because ______.”
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about my idea for the box.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 2 — A Time I Used My Imagination
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write about a time you used your imagination to solve a problem or feel better. What did you imagine? How did it help you? Use the simple past tense (imagined, allowed, felt).
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about a time i used my imagination.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 3 — My Opinion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Answer this opinion question in three sentences: “Is it good to share our feelings, even our sadness?” Say what you believe and give a reason.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about my opinion.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 4 — Connected Paragraph
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a six-sentence paragraph about the unit. Begin with a topic sentence and join ideas with and, but or because.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about connected paragraph.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 5 — Information Text
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Write a heading and five accurate facts. Use precise unit vocabulary and check spelling and punctuation.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about information text.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

writing.title: Writing 6 — Reflect and Improve
writing.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
writing.promptAndInstructions: Choose earlier writing, revise three sentences, add two useful details, and explain one improvement you made.
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 3 response uses complete connected sentences, precise unit words, and relevant details about reflect and improve.
writing.successCriteria: I answered the prompt; used precise Unit vocabulary; organised connected ideas; checked capitals and punctuation; reread and improved my work
writing.support: Plan three ideas, use the word bank, and say each sentence before writing.
writing.extension: Add a stronger opening, two precise details, and a concluding sentence.

activities.title: Activities 1 — True or False (Feelings and Ideas)
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write True or False. If it is false, write the correct answer.
An idea is a thought or plan in your mind.
Sadness is a feeling we should never talk about.
To imagine means to picture something in your mind.
A reason tells us why something happens.
Answers: 1. True   2. False (it is healthy and kind to share sadness)   3. True   4. True
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2 — Choose the Feeling Word
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Choose the best word: sincere, thoughtful, hopeless, or enjoyable.
The class trip to the sea was very ______. We had so much fun.
She gave a ______ apology, straight from her heart.
Bringing a gift for a sick friend is a ______ thing to do.
When I could not find my shoe, I felt ______.
Answers: 1. enjoyable   2. sincere   3. thoughtful   4. hopeless
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3 — Add the Reason
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Finish each sentence with a reason, using because.
I like to imagine, because ______.
I suggest we help the new student, because ______.
Sadness is not a bad thing, because ______.
Answers will be different for everyone. Ask your AI tutor to check that your reason makes sense.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4 — Vocabulary Sort and Explain
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Sort twelve unit words into useful categories. Explain the rule for each category in a complete sentence.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5 — Reading Evidence Hunt
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Find six important details in the unit texts. Record the detail and explain what it helps the reader understand.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6 — Create and Teach
activities.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion
activities.instructionsAndItems: Design a short game, diagram or demonstration that teaches one unit idea to another learner.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

assignments.title: Thinking, Feelings, and Imagination learning portfolio
assignments.instructions: Submit Writing 1 — My Idea for the Box and a recording of Speaking 1 — Share an Idea. Improve both pieces using the Grade 3 rubrics.
assignments.sourceFile: Derived from unit writing and speaking tasks

quizzes.question: What does 'idea' mean?
quizzes.options: A decision between two or more things. | A thought or plan in your mind. | The cause of something, or why it is done. | The reason why something is made or done.
quizzes.correctAnswer: A thought or plan in your mind.
quizzes.explanation: I have an idea for a fun game we can play.

quizzes.question: What does 'imagine' mean?
quizzes.options: A decision between two or more things. | A way to feel or understand something. | The reason why something is made or done. | To make a picture of something in your mind.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To make a picture of something in your mind.
quizzes.explanation: I can imagine a big, colourful dragon flying in the sky.

quizzes.question: What does 'reason' mean?
quizzes.options: A decision between two or more things. | A way to feel or understand something. | The cause of something, or why it is done. | The feeling you have when you are unhappy.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The cause of something, or why it is done.
quizzes.explanation: I have a reason for being late; my bus was slow.

quizzes.question: What does 'purpose' mean?
quizzes.options: A way to feel or understand something. | Something that happens to you that you remember. | The feeling you have when you are unhappy. | The reason why something is made or done.
quizzes.correctAnswer: The reason why something is made or done.
quizzes.explanation: The purpose of a pencil is to help us write.

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'Feelings Are Not Bad or Good': Sometimes we feel joy, and sometimes we feel _____.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Sometimes we feel joy, and sometimes we feel sadness.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Box of Ideas at School': “_____ something better,” I told him.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “Imagine something better,” I told him.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Box of Ideas': Every idea has a _____.” The next day, Isaac sat quietly under a tree in the schoolyard.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: Every idea has a reason.” The next day, Isaac sat quietly under a tree in the schoolyard.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Box of Ideas': “I don't even know its _____.” “Try again,” she said kindly.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “I don't even know its purpose.” “Try again,” she said kindly.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Box of Ideas': The teacher opened the box and read the first paper: “I want a garden where children feel peace.” “What a lovely _____,” said Amal.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: The teacher opened the box and read the first paper: “I want a garden where children feel peace.” “What a lovely choice,” said Amal.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

quizzes.question: Choose the missing word from 'The Box of Ideas': “Imagine something better.” Amal showed him what she had written: “One day I will write music with the _____ of rain and drums.
quizzes.explanation: The text says: “Imagine something better.” Amal showed him what she had written: “One day I will write music with the sense of rain and drums.
quizzes.origin: Generated from this unit's reading passages

teacherNotes.note: Model new language aloud, check meaning before independent work, use mixed-response modes, and give specific feedback on complete sentences, text evidence and vocabulary precision.

💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you read the Box of Ideas text with me and explain any hard words?”
“Can you play a listening game where I tell you what a person said?”
“Can you ask me questions about the feelings text to check I understood?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me practise sharing an idea and giving a reason?”
“Can you play the ‘Imagine It!’ game with me?”
“Can you help me talk about a feeling in a kind way?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my writing and tell me what to fix?”
“Can you help me add a reason with ‘because’?”
“Can you help me make my imagination story more exciting?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me use ‘idea’, ‘reason’, and ‘purpose’ in sentences?”
“Can you ask me to imagine something and describe it?”
“What is the difference between a ‘reason’ and a ‘purpose’?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you help me talk about my feelings in a kind way?”
“What is the difference between ‘sincere’ and ‘thoughtful’?”
“Can you give me the opposite of ‘hopeless’?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you practise a group discussion with me where we both suggest ideas?”
“How do I politely suggest an idea to my friends?”
💬  Ask Your AI Tutor
“Can you check my grammar answers?”
“Can you give me five more sentences with abstract nouns?”
“Can you help me change what someone said into reported speech?”

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.
answerKey.origin: Ehel English Content Template v1.1 curriculum expansion

answerKey.answerOrGuidance: A thought or plan in your mind. I have an idea for a fun game we can play.
To make a picture of something in your mind. I can imagine a big, colourful dragon flying in the sky.
The cause of something, or why it is done. I have a reason for being late; my bus was slow.
The reason why something is made or done. The purpose of a pencil is to help us write.
A decision between two or more things. You can have a cookie or an apple; it is your choice.
A way to feel or understand something. I had a sense that it might rain soon.
The feeling you have when you are unhappy. She felt sadness when her friend moved away.
Something that happens to you that you remember. I had a fun experience at the zoo.
Honest and truly meaning what you say. She gave me a sincere smile when I helped her.
Kind and caring about other people. She gave her friend a gift because she was thoughtful.

selfAssessment.statement: I can use abstract nouns like idea, reason, and sadness.

selfAssessment.statement: I can give a reason using “because”.

selfAssessment.statement: I can use “can” and “will” to talk about the future.

selfAssessment.statement: I can suggest an idea and let others speak.
